finder_output.summary: Both documents adopt a consent-driven, customer-permissioned model as the foundation for secure financial data sharing.
finder_output.label: aligns-with

finder_output.summary: Our framework mandates specific participation via a customer-count threshold, aligning with the mandatory-participation approach BIS notes some jurisdictions adopt.
finder_output.label: aligns-with
finder_output.scope_note: BIS observes mandatory participation is used but notes no evidence it is a necessary condition for a level playing field.

finder_output.summary: Both treat standardised APIs as the primary secure mechanism for open finance data exchange.
finder_output.label: aligns-with

finder_output.summary: Our framework is a regulatory-driven prescriptive approach with unified technical standards, matching one of the three approaches BIS classifies.
finder_output.label: aligns-with

finder_output.summary: Both emphasise financial inclusion as a core objective of open finance.
finder_output.label: aligns-with

finder_output.summary: We set detailed prescriptive requirements on consent lifecycle, deletion, and record-keeping, whereas BIS only discusses adoption evidence and general portability challenges without such operational rules.
finder_output.label: goes-beyond

finder_output.summary: We prescribe detailed customer protection, liability, and complaints-handling requirements, which BIS raises only as risks needing governance without specific rules.
finder_output.label: goes-beyond

finder_output.summary: BIS discusses data access pricing and reciprocity of access as an emerging policy stage, which our document only touches on via the principle of reciprocity without addressing pricing.
finder_output.label: silent-on

finder_output.summary: BIS highlights cross-border data portability and interoperability of open finance infrastructures, which our document mentions only aspirationally for national open data without operational requirements.
finder_output.label: silent-on

finder_output.summary: BIS notes payment initiation (write access) drives faster adoption, whereas our mandated scope focuses on read-only data sharing (document submission and account view) without payment initiation.
finder_output.label: silent-on

finder_output.summary: BIS presents international adoption evidence and structural/behavioural barriers to uptake, which our policy document does not address empirically.
finder_output.label: silent-on

critic_output.summary: Both documents adopt a consent-driven, customer-permissioned model as the foundation for secure financial data sharing.
critic_output.label: aligns-with
critic_output.scope_note: Our document operationalises this with binding specific/voluntary consent standards; BIS treats it as a descriptive feature of frameworks.

critic_output.summary: Our framework mandates specific participation via a customer-count threshold, aligning with the mandatory-participation approach BIS notes some jurisdictions adopt.
critic_output.label: aligns-with
critic_output.scope_note: BIS observes mandatory participation is used but notes no evidence it is a necessary condition for a level playing field.

critic_output.summary: Both treat standardised APIs as the primary secure mechanism for open finance data exchange.
critic_output.label: aligns-with
critic_output.scope_note: BIS anchor 7 describes screen scraping/reverse engineering; the API-standardisation argument is stronger against BIS Pap168 8 and 9. Kept but note the standardised-API framing sits in adjacent BIS anchors.

critic_output.summary: Our framework is a regulatory-driven prescriptive approach with unified technical standards, matching one of the three approaches BIS classifies.
critic_output.label: aligns-with
critic_output.scope_note: Dropped BIS Pap168 6 (which concerns data-monopoly origins, not approach classification); the prescriptive-approach taxonomy is the accurate anchor.

critic_output.summary: Both emphasise financial inclusion as a core objective of open finance.
critic_output.label: aligns-with

critic_output.summary: We set detailed prescriptive requirements on consent lifecycle, deletion, and record-keeping, whereas BIS only discusses adoption evidence and general portability challenges without such operational rules.
critic_output.label: goes-beyond

critic_output.summary: We prescribe detailed customer protection, liability, and complaints-handling requirements, which BIS raises only as risks needing governance without specific rules.
critic_output.label: goes-beyond

critic_output.summary: BIS discusses data access pricing and reciprocity of access as an emerging policy stage; our document invokes reciprocity as a participation principle but is silent on pricing/compensation for data access.
critic_output.label: silent-on
critic_output.scope_note: The pricing/FiDA reciprocity discussion is primarily in the unnumbered FiDA passage; BIS Pap168 24 covers cross-border interoperability. Our document offers no data-pricing regime.

critic_output.summary: BIS highlights cross-border data portability and interoperability of open finance infrastructures, which our document mentions only aspirationally for national open data without operational requirements.
critic_output.label: silent-on
critic_output.scope_note: Our document references regional data-sharing and global-standard alignment only as future collaboration, not as binding cross-border portability requirements.

critic_output.summary: BIS notes payment initiation (write access) drives faster adoption, whereas our mandated scope focuses on read-only data sharing (document submission and account view) without payment initiation.
critic_output.label: silent-on

critic_output.summary: BIS presents international adoption evidence and structural/behavioural barriers to uptake, which our policy document does not address empirically.
critic_output.label: silent-on

critic_output.summary: We require secure authentication (e.g. multi-factor) and technical/operational safeguards before granting data access; BIS notes banks retain control over authentication, monitoring and access management as an API design principle.
critic_output.label: aligns-with

critic_output.summary: We impose voluntary, non-bundled consent and prohibit coercing consent as a condition of service; BIS describes low willingness to share data and behavioural barriers but sets no anti-coercion rules.
critic_output.label: goes-beyond

critic_output.summary: We adopt a phased, multi-year mandated rollout with fixed commencement dates, aligning with BIS's observation that jurisdictions typically begin with open banking and phase in broader open finance over time.
critic_output.label: aligns-with

critic_output.summary: We prescribe binding cyber, API-security and operational-resilience controls for open finance activities; BIS identifies cyber threats and privacy breaches as risks to be governed but sets no specific control requirements.
critic_output.label: goes-beyond

critic_output.summary: We frame open finance as digital public infrastructure enabling national/regional data sharing and interoperability, consistent with BIS's characterisation of open finance as DPI built on shared API, identity, consent and governance layers.
critic_output.label: aligns-with
